sentences.text: Good morning, hello.
Good to see you.
Well, of course I was born in Bialystok, Poland.
And, my earliest memories are what a kid would remember growing up in a rather big town for Poland, but a small town in terms of the world today.
My parents were Yiddish schoolteachers.
I was not yet-- in my earliest memories, I was not yet of school age.
And while I recognized the fact that Jews were more or less persecuted, my life really didn't get impacted that way as a child.
I knew that every now and then, someone would shout a profanity that-- [POLISH] in Polish.
But I really didn't get involved, as a child anyway.
And so my life was very, very normal, of course, until September 1, 1939.
Actually, a little before that in preparation for World War II.
Our house was a hub of intellectual activity, because my parents were leaders in various movements within the Jewish society.

And so clearly, I do remember people coming to the house, sitting around the table, drinking tea, and discussing world events and the-- what was obviously coming was a big, black cloud that would be World War II.
And there were concerns.
And I recall from my own memory that when my father would talk to me about an impending war, I would visualize it as I did as a child, with warriors sword fighting in the streets.
But that's all I could imagine.
I didn't know about airplanes, and bombs, and things like that.
And-- and the warriors, I always thought, would be in the main streets.
They wouldn't come in our little street where we lived.
But that was the only way I visualized it.
And yes, I was conscious that something was afoot.
Well, yes I do, because it was a-- a big moment in my life.
I had spent my seven odd years of life in one place.

And suddenly, my father had decided that our bungalow, where we lived, was going to be unsafe during the bombings that might occur, whereas the building he had inherited from his father, which was on the outskirts of Bialystok, not in the industrial center, was a brick building, two story affair, and would be safer for us during this coming period of uncertainty and bombings.
And so we suddenly moved.
And I remember that quite vividly, because now I was suddenly living in an entirely different environment and neighborhood.
In fact, it was-- it was hardly a neighborhood.
There was a cemetery across the street that occupied most of my panorama that I was able to see as a child.
So yes, it was a big difference in my life.
They didn't, my father left.
We didn't.
What-- what had happened-- the story is this.
My father was one of the few, maybe only-- I'm not sure if there was another Jewish council member of the city, council of Bialystok.
But my father was a very prominent person in the city affairs and had been elected to the city council.
And may be the only Jew that was elected to the city council.
I'm not sure about that.
But as a member of the city council, he was called to a emergency meeting with the mayor of the city of Bialystock before war broke-- before the actual war-- strike that.
Before, actually, the Germans entered our city.
About a week before.
After war broke out, but before the Germans came into our city.

And the mayor advised the council members, Jew and non-Jew alike, that when the Germans would take over Bialystock, which was expected to happen, they would then arrest all the council members and use them as hostages, should anything go wrong in the city.
Should there be any violent protest or argument with the-- with the German army, or anything like that.
And they would use those hostages to shoot them, 10 at a time.
And therefore, the mayor advised that all the council personnel and-- and city councilmen should leave before the Germans entered the city.
And that, in fact, is what my father decided to do.
That wasn't till much later.
And if you like, I'll go into that whole history, but it-- it was quite a number of weeks later.
In fact, maybe a month later.
What-- what happened was, of course, that my father, on one evening, I was awoken in the middle of the night.
And my mother escorted me to a lot where there was this big van waiting with a lot of people milling around.
It was 3:00 in the morning.
On the way there, I remember the-- the sounds of the bombs and the [INAUDIBLE] fire that echoed through the buildings as we walked in Bialystok.
And I said my goodbyes to my father.
And of course, it was a very tearful event, as you can imagine.
A lot of people there saying goodbye to all the other councilmen that were leaving.
And my father left.
My mother and I stayed behind, of course.
And the Germans entered the city of Bialystok a few days later.
I remember there-- since we were on the outskirts of the city, I remember seeing the tanks enter, and then the army following the tanks, and so forth.
But eventually, we moved back to our bungalow, because there was no longer any bombings, and there was no longer any point in living away from the house.
No sooner did we come back to our own home in Bialystok that the Germans-- gestapo did come to search for my father.
He was, of course, gone.
And they searched through the clothes.
They asked lots of questions of my mother, and-- and so forth.
And it was quite a frightening affair.
But eventually, they left because my father just wasn't there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In fact, we didn't know where my father had left.
And we not-- we did not hear from him for the several weeks that the Germans occupied Bialystok.
And at some point in the history of these events, Stalin made a pact with Hitler to create a truce between the two countries and divide Poland, part to the Russians and part to the Germans.
Bialystock was supposed to be handed over to the Russians.
And so on a given Saturday afternoon when this was to happen, the German army marched out, and the Russian army marched in.
And I recall my mother taking me to the main street where these events which she told me would be quite historic were about to occur.
And of course, everyone was lined in the streets.
The entire Bialystok came out to watch.
And sure enough, the German army marched out.
And within an hour after that, the first of the Russian army started to march in.
And of course, the population of Bialystok was grateful.
In its way, the lesser of two evils was coming to take over Bialystok.
Certainly, the Russians were preferable to the Nazis, but only in terms of the lesser of two evils.
With the Russian army, however, came back all of those who had ran off before.

Every one of the city's hierarchy, the councilmen, the intellectuals, and so forth that had left in fear of the Germans returned, except two councilmen, my father and another gentleman, who did not trust the Russians any more than the Germans.

It is the-- that decision, among so many decisions that my father had to make that had to be absolutely right, to save our lives, and you couldn't make a mistake, was a critical decision in saving the [INAUDIBLE].

Because sure enough, within two days after all of these people returned, the NKVD, which is the precursor to the KGB, came to search for my father no differently than had the gestapo some weeks before.
Had my father returned, we would have been arrested and every one of those people that were arrested were sent off to Siberia.
And most of them were never heard from again.
That was the lucky fate was to be sent off to Siberia.
Who knows what else might have occurred.
But my father did not return.
In fact, my mother and I didn't know where he was.
We heard nothing.

And when his friends came to our house to-- to explain to my mother why my father didn't return, they said to her that he was a very stubborn man who didn't want to listen to good reason when others tried to tell him to come back with them.
And of course, they found out to their dismay that he was right and they were wrong when the arrests began.
It wasn't until weeks later that suddenly a neighbor of ours who had a telephone-- that was a rare commodity among Jews in Poland.
You had to be very wealthy to own the telephone-- got a telephone call, and came rushing to our house to ask my mother-- to tell her that my father was on the phone and wanted to talk with her.
And my mother, of course, ran off to get the call.
When she came back, it was the first time she felt the mixture of relief and trepidation again, because my father was well and was alive.
But now our trek of running began in real terms.

My father had found out that Vilna, the city of Vilna, that it was always in a tug-of-war between Poland and Lithuania as to who owned Vilna, was going to be turned over to the Lithuanians by the Russians.
And therefore, Vilna would be a safe place for my father to reunite with his family if my mother and I would take the last immediate train out of Bialystock to Vilna and meet him there.
Because there, he would be safe.
The reason he didn't feel that he was safe under the Bolsheviks was that he was an outspoken anti-Communist.
He was a member of the Bundists, which was a socialist movement, of course.
But socialism and Communism were eons apart.
And in his view, the Communists were not to be trusted any more than the Nazis.
And so he felt in Lithuania he would be free to return, that is, and reunite with us.
And my mother and I hurriedly packed, left for the train station that very night.
It was the last train out of Bialystock.
And it-- it normally was a several hour ride to Vilna from Bialystock by train.
It took over a whole night.

It was a scene that I never forgot, and never will forget, of the-- the station house with all the refugees running, and packs, and baggage, and people shouting and arguing and trying to get aboard this train, which we got on board.
My mother sat on a box the entire ride.
I was lucky because I was a little kid and they gave me the window seat.
And so I was able to sit at the window for hours at a time.
Much of the time, the train kind of stopped during the night.
And you would hear shouts.
You would hear gunfire from time to time.
But eventually, it ended in-- in Vilna.
And I saw, for a fleeting moment, my father running about at the station in his [POLISH], you know, it's a hat.
Oh, a [POLISH] is a men's hat.
There's nothing to describe.
[LAUGHS] It's the standard of man's hat, very popular in Europe, of course.
And my father wore it.
And there he was, looking about with his eyes, screening the windows of the train to see if he could spot us.
I spotted him.
Then he disappeared, it was just a fleeting moment.
We eventually caught up with each other.
And that was the first of the trek across two thirds of the world that brought us to the United States.
We lived in Vilna almost a year.
Well, we must have arrived late October, 1939.
Or early November, I don't have a specific date in my mind.
And we stayed there through December of 1940.
So it was something over a year that we lived in Vilna.
Well, there were two portions of time.
In the beginning, when the Lithuanians were the governing power of Vilna, he lived with us.
We got an apartment, a one-- sort of one room apartment overlooking [INAUDIBLE].

It's interesting I mentioned [INAUDIBLE] because [INAUDIBLE] ended up to be on the street on which the headquarters of the uprising of the ghetto eventually was there, only a few doorways, in fact from where we lived on [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] was deep within the Jewish quarter, of course, of Vilna.
It was actually the butcher's street, so that, you know, during the normal times, this was a hub of activity which butcher shops and people coming, and milling, and buying, and-- and so forth.
And we lived on-- on the second floor overlooking this street with a little balcony that I would stand in front of from time to time.
The circumstances were a little different though, because now suddenly, instead of learning Polish, I was learning Lithuanian.
So this was my actual third language in my seven and a half years of life, Yiddish, Polish, and now Lithuanian.
But it was more or less normal.
I was with kids my age, kids on the street.
I-- I learned the facts of life.
I, I had friends.
We had birthday parties.
And while we were refugees, the community took us in because my parents are well-known, respected individuals.
And we immediately became part of the social sphere of Jewish life in Vilna.
But that only lasted until the Russians changed their mind, took back not only Vilna, but all of Lithuania, as you know.
And suddenly, my father was on the lam again.
And now he left again for the forests of Lithuania outside of Kaunas.
Again, he was in the company of others who were running, and for one reason or other didn't trust the-- the Russians.

It was there in Kaunas, in the forests of Kaunas, that rumors came to him that the General Council of Japan, Sugihara, might be issuing visas to enter-- a transit visa, transit papers, in effect-- to enter Japan.
No one knew if you could leave anyway, because to leave a Russian occupied territory, you needed permission from the government.
But in those days, my father explained to me more than one occasion, a transit visa was your only hope that maybe you could get out and escape.
And that's where Sugihara came in.
Well, he told me, on more than one occasion, told me that, you know, word came to them.
They were living in the forest.
They were the refugees.
And they were Bundists.
And Bundists were comrades in arms, in effect.
And everything that happened anywhere in the world, there was a network of information to travel to refugees.
Refugees are well, very well-informed.
By-- by instinct, and the nature of their circumstance, they have to be to survive.
And they learned that this ambassador-- not ambassador, but the Council General of Japan-- was going to issue perhaps visas.

And he, with so many others of course, took their life in their own hands to go to Sugihara's home, where the foreign ministry of Japan lived, existed, and milled about the courtyard-- there are many pictures of this scene that I have seen since-- pleading with Sugihara to issue them transit visas.
Oh yes, oh yes, stood there, and went, and waited.
He told me for hours and days, because it didn't happen right away.
Days on end, until finally he was one of the fortunate ones among some 5,000 or more-- no one knows the exact figure-- that received visa.
And of course, if you received the visa, it was a visa for your entire family.
So every time Sugihara issued one visa to Moshe Melamdovich he issued it to Moshe Melundelvich's wife, Fejgla Melamdovich and their son, [?
Label ?] Melamdovich as well.
Yes.
Well, he-- he mentioned the fact that the council was a-- a kindly, young man, he said, but seemed to understand the plight of the Jews that were in the courtyard and around the home.
And he-- he entered alone because they took turns going in.
You had to sign papers, and so forth.
And there wasn't a great deal of conversation, because it was all done quite hurriedly.
There were so many waiting.
And you had to do the formalities as quickly as possible.

But in that moment, my father said, perhaps because here was a savior giving him a visa, or perhaps because he really saw the inside of Sugihara, he said to me, he looked like a person with a great deal of compassion.
And of course, since then I know the story so much better.
But I recall my father's words as a child to me, later saying that this was a compassionate man saving the lives of so many people.
Of course, at that time we didn't know that it would end up saving our lives.
It was just a hope.
We still had to get permission to leave Lithuania, in effect to leave Russia.
And that became the most dangerous thing my father had to decide to do or not do.
I, I presume he did, although I didn't have any conversations.
Remember now, I am eight years old at this time, and I wasn't getting all the details and secrets of his mind.
But I was getting a lot, because my parents kind of treated me as an adult.
I was their only offspring, and they believed, as intellectuals often do, that children should know everything.
So in effect, I was getting as much as they thought I ought to have, and it was quite a bit.
No.
I heard the word, 'the Dutch' mentioned, so that I knew there was an involvement with the Dutch embassy as well.
But no specifics that I remember.
None that I ever saw.
No.
Our visa was at the courtesy of the transit visa from Japan to [INAUDIBLE], yes.
But I don't know of it.
As a child, I never knew of any other details than that.
Well in later years, when it became quite evident-- when we arrived in the United States and so many others did as a consequence of Sugihara-- we had many conversations that this was a great man.

That this man, in contravention of orders from the government of Japan, chose to follow his conscience rather than the dictates of government and issue visas to people who are desperate, and that this was their only way out.

Clearly, my father considered him a great man, 'a great humanitarian,' in his words, who had saved the lives of countless and countless people, and their families, and in effect their offspring, and so forth.
He remembered his name.
He remembered that the name was Sugihara.
He didn't remember his first name.
It wasn't until years later, when I understood what that was and the whole story of it.
But my father did remember Sugihara's name.
We had the passport, remember.
His signature was on it.
Sugihara's signature is on the passport.
Both.
I believe it to be both.
At least, that's my memory of it.
And I haven't looked at it in a long time.
Actually, not a visa.
What we got was papers that allowed us to leave.
It was under Stalin's sort of orders.
He was just happy to get rid of refugees.
And anyone that had a visa to-- to go somewhere, like this transit visa to Japan, that applied for permission had a chance.
But when you apply for permission, of course you have to fill out all the papers and all the documents.
And you have to explain, in fact, where you're from and why you want to leave Russia's good offices.

That was dangerous for my father, because if the ministry of, the foreign ministry of Russia, connected up the fact that we weren't really running from the Nazis anymore, that our home was Bialystock, and that in effect my father was running from the Russians, that meant Siberia at best.
And so the decision to, whether to apply for permission to leave or not was a decision we made as a family.
I was taken into that-- those proceedings.
Of course, I voted with my parents, who both voted to take the chance and make the application.

And then the hope was, as my father said, in this confusion, in this moment of confusion, knowing as he knew the Russian bureaucracy and inefficiencies anyway, they would never-- they would never connect up the Melamdovich who was the staunch anti-Communist who had written a great deal of material about being an anti-Communist and this Melamdovich who had a visa to get to Japan.
And in effect, take the chance.
He took the chance.
Oh, months.
We, my mother and I, would go every Friday to see the names on the ministry in Vilna, without my father who was still in hiding, to see if our name showed up.
And it was several months before finally, one day, we went, and there was our name, among many others.
Oh no, oh no.
He never showed up again after we submitted the papers.
That wasn't part of the process.
Part of the process, my mother could come and answer questions.
And she did.
But my father was very much afraid to show up.
In fact, he had shaved his head, so that he looked entirely different than the father I knew.
And the passport which we got was with a shaved head, you know, my father looking not like himself.
Yeah.
